query: When did the last king from Britain's House of Hanover die?
responses:
The last king from Britain's House of Hanover was George V. He reigned as the King of Hanover from November 18, 1851, to September 20, 1866 [[1]][[2]]. King George V died on January 20, 1936. His death occurred at Sandringham House in Norfolk, England [[4]][[5]][[6]]. He had been suffering from chronic lung issues since 1928 and his health had gradually declined over the previous few months [[5]]. The exact cause of his death was a mixture of cocaine and morphine administered by his physician, Lord Bertrand Dawson, who intended to grant him a painless death [[7]][[8]].

Thus, the last king from Britain's House of Hanover died on January 20, 1936.

query: When did the president who set the precedent of a two term limit enter office?
responses:
The president who set the precedent of a two-term limit in the United States is George Washington. This precedent was established in 1796, when Washington decided not to seek a third term as president. This decision was made several months before the election and set an unofficial standard that presidents should only serve for two terms [[1]][[2]].

George Washington entered office on April 30, 1789. This date marks the day of his first inauguration as the inaugural president of the United States [[4]][[5]][[6]]. His decision not to seek a third term in 1796 further solidified this precedent, which has since been codified into law with the passage of the Twenty-second Amendment to the U.S. Constitution in 1951 [[7]].

In summary, George Washington set the precedent for a two-term limit in US presidential elections by deciding not to seek a third term after his first inauguration on April 30, 1789 [[1]][[2]][[4]][[5]][[6]].

query: When did the president who set the precedent of a two term limit leave office?
responses:
George Washington, the first President of the United States, set a precedent for a two-term limit as President. In his Farewell Address in 1796, Washington announced that he would not seek re-election for a third term [[1]][[5]]. This decision was influenced by his desire to encourage future presidents to follow the same practice and avoid the potential pitfalls of long-term leadership [[2]]. 

Washington's decision was significant because it established an unwritten rule that presidents should serve no more than two terms. This precedent was later formalized in the 22nd Amendment to the U.S. Constitution, which limits presidents to two four-year terms [[3]][[4]].

Washington left office on March 4, 1797. This date marks the end of his second term as President. Washington had served from April 30, 1789, to March 4, 1797[[7]][[8]].

In conclusion, George Washington set a precedent for a two-term limit as President and left office on March 4, 1797 [[1]][[2]][[3]][[4]][[5]][[7]][[8]].

In summary, the death toll from the second most powerful earthquake ever recorded—the 1964 Alaska earthquake—was around 139 people [[6]][[7]].

query: When did the person who gave the Checkers speech die?
responses:
The person who gave the Checkers speech was Richard Nixon, the 37th President of the United States. The Checkers speech, also known as the "Fund Speech," was delivered by Nixon on September 23, 1952 [[1]][[2]][[3]]. In this speech, Nixon addressed allegations that he had misused campaign funds and explained that he had received $18,000 from a group of supporters to cover personal expenses related to his political activities. He emphasized that none of this money was used for his personal benefit and that it was used to defray political expenses not covered by government funds [[2]][[4]].

Richard Nixon died on April 22, 1994. He passed away at the age of 81 after suffering a significant stroke four days prior [[8]][[9]][[10]].

query: When was the philosopher that formulated the hard problem of consciousness born?
responses:
David Chalmers, the philosopher who formulated the hard problem of consciousness, was born on April 20, 1966 [[12]][[13]][[14]]. This information confirms that Chalmers is indeed the philosopher in question and provides his birthdate.

Chalmers is a prominent figure in the field of philosophy, particularly known for his contributions to the philosophy of mind and consciousness. His work has had a significant impact on various areas of philosophy, including free will, knowledge, value, and life [[1]][[2]][[3]][[4]][[5]][[6]].

The hard problem of consciousness refers to explaining why and how humans and other organisms have qualia (subjective experiences) or subjective consciousness [[10]]. Chalmers argued against physicalism in the philosophy of mind with his argument that even if all mental states are identical to physical states (physicalism), there would still be an unexplained "gap" between these states [[3]]. This argument supports dualism, which posits that mental states have properties that are not reducible to physical properties.

Chalmers proposed that consciousness is determined by functional organization—that any two systems with the same fine-grained functional organization will have qualitatively identical experiences [[9]]. This principle suggests that understanding consciousness requires understanding its functional structure rather than its material composition.

While not exclusively focused on free will, Chalmers' work touches upon it through his exploration of compatibilist views on free will and deterministic accounts [[7]][[8]]. He also discusses how free will might be compatible with certain theories about consciousness.

In summary, David Chalmers' contributions to philosophy span across multiple domains but are most notably recognized for addressing fundamental questions about the nature of conscious experience and its relationship to physical processes[[1]][[2]][[3]][[4]][[5]][[6]].

Its ability to maintain high speeds over long periods contributes significantly to its survival and reproductive success in various environments [[3]][[6]]. The White-Throated Needletail Swift's speed record highlights the remarkable adaptations that have evolved in birds to optimize their flight capabilities.

In conclusion, the third fastest bird in terms of airspeed is the White-Throated Needletail Swift, capable of reaching speeds up to 105 mph (169 kph) [[8]][[9]].

responses:
The first location of Starbucks, the world's largest coffeehouse chain, was opened on March 30, 1971. This significant event took place at Pike Place Market in Seattle [[1]][[2]][[3]][[4]]. The founders of Starbucks, Jerry Baldwin, Zev Siegl, and Gordon Bowker, initially set up their store with the intention of selling fresh-roasted coffee beans and other specialty items like tea and spices. However, they quickly realized that customers preferred to enjoy their purchases on-site rather than taking them home [[1]]. This realization led to the transformation of Starbucks into a full-service coffeehouse where customers could enjoy their beverages in a cozy atmosphere.

Over time, Starbucks expanded its operations beyond Seattle. In 1987, it rebranded itself as a coffeehouse and began its expansion across North America. By the late 1990s and early 2000s, Starbucks had established itself as a dominant player in the global coffee market with stores in Japan (1996), Europe (1998), and China (1999) [[1]].

In summary, Starbucks' first location was opened on March 30, 1971 at Pike Place Market in Seattle. This event marked not only the inception of one of the world's most iconic brands but also laid down its foundation for future growth into an international powerhouse within the beverage industry.

query: What rocket was used for the mission that landed the first humans on the moon?
responses:
The mission that landed the first humans on the Moon was Apollo 11. This historic event took place on July 20, 1969, when Commander Neil Armstrong and Lunar Module Pilot Buzz Aldrin successfully landed the Apollo Lunar Module Eagle on the lunar surface [[1]][[2]][[3]]. 

Apollo 11 was a significant milestone in human space exploration, marking the culmination of extensive efforts by NASA and its international partners. The mission aimed to achieve President John F. Kennedy's goal of landing a man on the Moon and returning him safely to Earth before the end of the decade [[2]][[5]]. 

The landing occurred at approximately 20:17 UTC (Coordinated Universal Time) and was followed by Neil Armstrong's famous words: "That's one small step for [a] man, one giant leap for mankind" as he became the first person to set foot on another celestial body [[1]][[4]]. 

Overall, Apollo 11 remains a pivotal moment in human history, symbolizing human ingenuity and determination in exploring beyond our planetary boundaries.

The rocket used for the Apollo 11 mission was the Saturn V. This rocket was developed by NASA under the Apollo program specifically for human exploration of the Moon. The Saturn V carried all Apollo lunar missions, including Apollo 11, which successfully landed astronauts Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin on the Moon on July 20, 1969 [[7]][[8]][[9]].

In summary:
- **Mission**: The mission that landed humans on moon is **Apollo 11**.
- **Rocket**: The rocket used for this mission is **Saturn V**.

Thus, it can be concluded that Saturn V rocket played a crucial role in achieving this monumental feat in space exploration history [[1]][[2]][[3]].

query: Can people who have celiac eat camel meat?
responses:
People with celiac disease can safely consume camel meat. Celiac disease is an autoimmune disorder where the ingestion of gluten, a protein found in wheat, barley, and rye, leads to damage in the small intestine [[1]][[2]]. Therefore, individuals with celiac disease must strictly avoid foods that contain gluten.

Camel meat does not contain gluten. Gluten is a protein found in wheat, barley, and rye; there is no evidence that camel meat contains these proteins [[8]][[9]]. This makes camel meat a suitable option for individuals with celiac disease or gluten intolerance.

The typical processing methods used for camel meats include grilling, roasting, braising, frying, and smoking [[10]][[11]]. These methods help preserve the nutritional values and enhance the taste of camel meats without introducing any gluten through cross-contamination.

In summary:
- Camel meat does not contain gluten.
- The typical processing methods used for camel meats (grilling roasting braising frying smoking) do not introduce any gluten through cross-contamination.
- Therefore people with celiac disease can safely consume camel meat[[8]][[9]][[10]][[11]].

Thus it is confirmed that people who have celiac can eat camel meat.

query: When did the war that Neil Armstrong served in end?
responses:
Neil Armstrong served in the United States Navy during World War II. He joined the Navy in 1949 and continued to serve until 1952, flying combat missions up until that time [[1]][[2]]. During his service, he was primarily a carrier-based fighter pilot [[3]].

World War II officially ended on September 2, 1945. This date marks the formal surrender of Japan aboard the USS Missouri in Tokyo Bay, which was signed by representatives of the Japanese government and accepted by representatives of the Allied powers [[6]][[8]][[11]]. 

However, it is important to note that World War II effectively ended earlier in Europe. On May 8, 1945 (Victory in Europe Day or V-E Day), Germany unconditionally surrendered to the Allies [[7]][[9]]. Due to the time difference with Soviet forces, they celebrated their "Victory Day" on May 9 [[8]].

Thus, while September 2, 1945 is recognized as the official end date for World War II globally due to Japan's surrender and formal signing of documents aboard the USS Missouri [[6]][[10]], Europe had already experienced its end with Germany's unconditional surrender on May 8 [[7]][[9]].

In conclusion:
- Neil Armstrong served in World War II from 1949 to 1952.
- The war officially ended on September 2, 1945.
- However, Europe experienced its end earlier on May 8, 1945[[1]][[2]][[3]][[6][7][8][9][10][11]].

query: What is the nickname of the easternmost U.S. state?

The easternmost U.S. state is Maine. The nickname of Maine is "The Pine Tree State" [[1]]. This nickname reflects the state's abundant pine forests and its historical significance in the logging industry.

Despite this isolation, Maine has a rich history and culture that are celebrated through various nicknames.

In addition to being known as "The Pine Tree State," Maine is also referred to as "Vacationland" due to its numerous attractions such as Acadia National Park, which offers stunning coastal views and hiking trails [[3]]. The state's rugged coastline, quaint fishing villages, and charming seaside towns further contribute to its allure as a vacation destination.

Overall, Maine's nickname of "The Pine Tree State" encapsulates both its natural beauty and historical significance.

query: Who succeeded the longest reigning Roman emperor?
responses:
The longest reigning Roman Emperor was Augustus, who ruled from 27 BCE until his death in 14 CE. His reign marked the beginning of the Roman Empire and is often considered a period of peace and stability, known as the Pax Romana [[1]][[2]][[3]].

Tiberius succeeded Augustus as the Roman Emperor. Tiberius, born in 42 BC, was adopted by Augustus and became his stepson. He ruled from AD 14 until his death in AD 37 [[6]][[7]][[5]].

This information confirms that the University of Oxford's official color is indeed "Oxford Blue," which plays a significant role in representing this prestigious institution both within academia and beyond.

query: In 2016, who was the host of the longest running talk show?
responses:
In 2016, the host of the longest running talk show was Jimmy Fallon. He took over as the host of "The Tonight Show" on February 17, 2014 [[11]]. This iconic late-night talk show has been a staple in American television since its debut in 1954 and has seen several hosts over the years, including Johnny Carson (from 1954 to 1992), Jay Leno (from 1992 to 2009), and Conan O'Brien (from 2010 to 2015) [[8]][[9]].

"The Tonight Show" is known for its monologue segments, celebrity interviews, and musical performances. It has consistently been one of the top-rated late-night programs in America and holds the title of the world's longest-running talk show [[1]][[3]]. In addition to Jimmy Fallon's hosting duties, other notable figures associated with "The Tonight Show" include Stephen Colbert (host from September 2005 to February 2014) and Jay Leno (host from April to September of that year) [[8]][[9]].

Thus, by breaking down the question into sub-questions and conducting thorough searches using a web search graph approach, we have determined that Jimmy Fallon was indeed the host of "The Tonight Show" during its longest running period in history.

query: Who is the predecessor of the longest-reigning British monarch?
responses:
The longest-reigning British monarch is Queen Elizabeth II. She ascended to the throne on February 6, 1952, and reigned until her death on September 8, 2022. Her reign lasted for a total of 70 years and seven months [[1]][[2]][[3]][[4]]. 

Queen Elizabeth II surpassed the previous record held by her great-great-grandmother, Queen Victoria, who reigned for approximately 63 years and seven months [[2]]. Therefore, Queen Victoria is the predecessor of Queen Elizabeth II as the longest-reigning British monarch.

Queen Elizabeth II's predecessor was King Edward VIII. He abdicated the throne in December 1936, making his younger brother, George VI, the new king. This event marked the end of Edward VIII's reign and the beginning of Queen Elizabeth II's lineage to the British monarchy [[6]][[7]][[8]].

Thus, King Edward VIII is identified as the predecessor of Queen Elizabeth II among all British monarchs.

query: In what year was the country that is the third largest exporter of coffee founded?

This event was a result of a series of uprisings and battles led by Simón Bolívar and other independence leaders, culminating in the Congress of Angostura in 1819 [[15]][[16]].

Therefore, the year when Colombia, the third largest exporter of coffee, was founded is **1819**.

responses.mississippi_river:
One of the deepest rivers in North America is the Snake River, which forms part of Hells Canyon. Hells Canyon is recognized as North America's deepest river gorge, with a depth of 7,993 feet (2,436 meters) [[3]][[4]][[8]]. This depth makes it significantly deeper than other notable river gorges in the region.

The Snake River itself is not only deep but also one of the major rivers in North America. It flows through Idaho and Oregon and eventually joins with the Columbia River [[7]]. The depth of Hells Canyon contributes to its status as a significant geographical feature and a popular destination for outdoor activities such as whitewater rafting and jet boating [[3]].

In summary, while there are several deep rivers in North America, the Snake River within Hells Canyon stands out as one of the deepest due to its impressive gorge depth.
responses.amazone_river:
The Amazon River is one of the longest rivers in the world, with estimates varying slightly depending on the source. The most commonly cited length is approximately 4,225 miles (6,800 kilometers) from its source to its mouth in the Atlantic Ocean [[1]][[3]]. This measurement makes it the second-longest river in the world after the Nile River [[1]].

However, there are reports suggesting that its length could be even longer. Some sources indicate that it might be as long as 7,025 to 7,100 kilometers (4,365 to 4,410 miles) [[1]]. These variations primarily stem from differing definitions of where exactly the river begins and ends.

Despite these slight discrepancies in length measurements, there is no doubt that the Amazon River is a significant and influential waterway. It not only supports vast biodiversity but also plays a crucial role in global climate regulation through its extensive rainforest ecosystem [[3]].
responses.yangtze_river:
The Yangtze River, also known as the Chang Jiang in Chinese, is the longest river in both China and Asia. It has a total length of approximately 6,300 kilometers (3,915 miles) [[0]][[1]][[2]]. This makes it the third-longest river in the world after the Nile and Amazon rivers.

The Yangtze River originates from Jari Hill in the Tanggula Mountains of Tibet and flows eastward through several provinces before emptying into the East China Sea. Along its course, it serves as a significant waterway for transportation and irrigation [[0]][[1]].

In summary, the length of the Yangtze River is approximately 6,300 kilometers (3,915 miles), making it one of the longest rivers globally.

The highest elevation in New Guinea is Puncak Jaya, also known as Carstensz Pyramid, which reaches an elevation of 4,884 meters (16,023 feet) [[6]][[7]]. This peak is located in the western half of the island and is considered the highest point in Oceania.

Thus, the highest elevation of the second largest island in the world is \(\boxed{4,884}\) meters.

query: The third largest river (by discharge) in the world is in what countries?
responses:
The third largest river by discharge in the world is the Congo River. This river flows through several countries in Central Africa, primarily including:

1. **Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC)**: The Congo River originates in northeastern Zambia and flows into the DRC, where it forms a significant part of its territory. The DRC is home to a large portion of the Congo River basin [[6]][[7]][[8]].

2. **Republic of the Congo**: This country is located on the southern bank of the Congo River, forming its southern border with Angola and Gabon [[6]][[7]].

3. **Angola**: Angola's northern border is shared with Zambia and Namibia, where tributaries flow into the main river system [[6]][[7]].

4. **Central African Republic (CAR)**: The CAR lies along parts of both banks of the upper reaches of the river [[6]][[10]].

5. **Tanzania**: Although not directly connected by a continuous flow, some tributaries from Tanzania contribute to its overall water volume [[9]].

6. **Burundi**: Similar to Tanzania, Burundi's waters indirectly contribute to those flowing into the main course via tributaries [[9]].

7. **Rwanda**: Rwanda also has tributaries that feed into larger bodies contributing to this vast river system[[9]]. 

These countries collectively form an extensive network around one of Africa's most significant rivers by discharge volume[[8]].

Thus, the year when Best Buy, which was originally founded as Sound of Music, was added to the S&P 500 is **1999**.

query: When did the war that Neil Armstrong served in end?
responses:
Neil Armstrong, the astronaut who famously walked on the moon as part of the Apollo 11 mission, served in World War II. However, it is important to clarify that he did not serve during World War II in a military capacity related to space exploration or the moon landing. Instead, his service was in a different context.

During World War II, Neil Armstrong served as a naval aviator in the United States Navy. He joined the Navy in 1949 and continued to serve until 1952 [[1]][[2]][[3]]. During this period, he flew combat missions during the Korean War [[2]]. Specifically, he was involved in 78 combat missions as a pilot of the F-9F Panther jet fighter [[2]]. These missions were crucial for his development as an aviator and contributed to his later career in space exploration [[4]].

Armstrong's experience during these combat missions is significant because it provided him with valuable skills and knowledge that he later applied to his role as an astronaut. His time in the Navy also instilled discipline and a strong work ethic that would be essential for his future endeavors [[5]].

In summary, Neil Armstrong's role during World War II was primarily focused on serving as a naval aviator, where he flew numerous combat missions during the Korean War. This experience played a critical part in shaping his career path and preparing him for future challenges, including becoming an astronaut and eventually walking on the moon.

The official end date for World War II across all theaters was confirmed with Japan's formal surrender on September 2, 1945[[10]][[11]][[12]]. This event is commonly referred to as V-J Day (Victory over Japan Day), marking the official end of hostilities worldwide following Japan's unconditional surrender aboard the USS Missouri in Tokyo Bay[[10]][[11]][[12]].

Thus, while September 2, 1945 is recognized globally due to Japan's surrender document being signed that day by representatives of both Allied forces and Japan[[10]][[11]], it should be noted that significant hostilities had already ceased earlier by May 8th when Germany unconditionally surrendered[[11]]. The official end date for World War II across all theaters was confirmed with Japan's formal surrender on September 2[[10]].
